statement: Judge-gym should use a layered rate-limit design in the Temporal rewrite: queue partitioning plus Temporal queue-level dispatch caps for coarse cross-worker shaping, worker concurrency controls for host protection, and a Redis-backed distributed limiter only for provider/model quotas that require shared request and token accounting across workers.
rationale: The current system enforces request, input-token, output-token, and batch scopes per model. Temporal natively handles queue dispatch and worker saturation, but not shared provider token budgets. A layered design preserves the existing business behavior without overusing Redis for everything.
keyAssumptions: Judge-gym will continue to care about per-model request and token quotas after the rewrite
Temporal task queues can be kept coarse enough that queue sprawl does not become an operational burden
An external Redis dependency is acceptable if it is limited to quota enforcement rather than general orchestration
validationCriteria: Multiple workers cannot collectively exceed a shared provider/model token budget even when they scale horizontally
Queue-level dispatch throttling can be changed centrally without worker option thrash
The design distinguishes host-protection limits from provider-quota limits
critiquePoints: If v0 runs on a single worker deployment, the Redis layer may be unnecessary overhead at first
Batch APIs may require a different reservation/reconciliation policy than synchronous chat calls
questions: Which quota dimensions should reserve estimated output tokens before a call, and which should reconcile only after actual usage is known?
Is a single Redis-backed token bucket algorithm enough, or do some scopes want a sliding-window approximation instead?
